title: Contributing to VisualMath
text:
Thank you for your interest in contributing to VisualMath! We welcome contributions from everyone, whether you are developing interactive JavaScript visualizations, structuring HTML pages, creating Quarto documents, or writing Python notebooks.


By contributing to this repository, you agree that your contributions will be licensed under the project’s Apache License 2.0.
Because copyright in this project is distributed, you retain ownership of your code, but you grant the community a perpetual, royalty-free license to use, modify, and distribute it under the Apache 2.0 terms.




To maintain consistency and ensure everyone receives proper academic and legal credit, every new code or content file added to this repository must include a copyright header at the very top.
Please copy, paste, and update the template matching your file’s language:


For Markdown and Quarto files, wrap the header in standard HTML comment tags at the very beginning of the document:
<!--
Copyright (c) [Year] [Your Name]. All rights reserved.
Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.
Authors: [Your Name]
-->



For Jupyter Notebooks, create a Markdown cell at the very top of your notebook and paste the following:
> **Copyright (c) [Year] [Your Name]. All rights reserved.**  
> *Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.*  
> **Authors:** [Your Name]



/**
 * Copyright (c) [Year] [Your Name]. All rights reserved.
 * Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.
 * Authors: [Your Name]
 */



# Copyright (c) [Year] [Your Name]. All rights reserved.
# Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.
# Authors: [Your Name]



<!--
Copyright (c) [Year] [Your Name]. All rights reserved.
Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.
Authors: [Your Name]
-->






Minor edits: If you are fixing a bug, adjusting layout spacing, or correcting a typo in an existing file, you do not need to alter the header.
Significant additions: If you contribute a substantial new feature, script, or section to an existing file, please add your name to the Authors line of that file:

Authors: Apurva Nakade, [Your Name]

title: Fixed Point Iteration
Fixed Point Iteration
text:
What the picture is showing
The plot shows two curves:

the function \(y=g(x)\),
the line \(y=x\).

The red path shows the iteration. From the current point, the method moves vertically to the curve \(y=g(x)\), then horizontally to the line \(y=x\). Repeating this creates the cobweb pattern.
This visualization is useful because it shows whether the iteration appears to be moving toward a fixed point, moving away from it, or oscillating.
A common condition that helps guarantee local convergence is
\[
|g'(x)|<1
\]
near the fixed point.

title: Newton’s Method
Newton’s Method
text:
What the picture is showing
At the current value \(x_n\), Newton’s method draws the tangent line to the graph of \(y=f(x)\).
The tangent line at \(x_n\) is
\[
y=f(x_n)+f'(x_n)(x-x_n).
\]
The next approximation \(x_{n+1}\) is where this tangent line crosses the \(x\)-axis.
Setting \(y=0\) gives
\[
0=f(x_n)+f'(x_n)(x_{n+1}-x_n).
\]
Solving for \(x_{n+1}\) gives the Newton update:
\[
x_{n+1}=x_n-\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}.
\]
Newton’s method can converge very quickly when the starting point is close to the root and the derivative at the root is not zero. But it can also fail if the derivative is too small or if the starting point is not chosen well.

title: Root Finding
text:
Root finding methods are numerical techniques for solving equations of the form \(f(x)=0\). These methods are central in computational mathematics because many problems cannot be solved exactly with algebra alone.

title: Bisection Method
Bisection Method
text:
What the picture is showing
The blue curve is the graph of \(f(x)\). The black horizontal line is \(y=0\).
The shaded blue region is the current interval \([a,b]\). The blue points show the endpoint values \((a,f(a))\) and \((b,f(b))\).
The red point is the midpoint \((m,f(m))\). Each step halves the interval and keeps the side that still contains a sign change.

title: VisualMath
text:
Interactive Computational Mathematics


VisualMath


Explore numerical methods through fast, interactive, browser-based visualizations. VisualMath turns abstract computational mathematics algorithms into dynamic, step-by-step apps.


Start exploring Learn more










\(f(x)=0\)


\({x_{n+1}=x_n-\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}}\)


\({m=\frac{a+b}{2}}\)






Available Modules


Choose a topic


Each module contains interactive apps, visual explanations, and step-by-step numerical computations.




  √
  Root Finding
  
    Explore interactive methods for solving equations of the form \(f(x)=0\),
    including bisection, fixed point iteration, and Newton’s method.
  
  
    Bisection
    Fixed Point
    Newton
  


  ∫
  Numerical Integration
  
    Coming soon: visualize how numerical rules approximate areas and definite integrals.
  
  
    Coming soon
  


  Ax
  Linear Algebra
  
    Coming soon: interactive visualizations for matrix operations, systems, and transformations.
  
  
    Coming soon
  






Why VisualMath?


Designed for exploration





Step-by-step algorithms


Move through each iteration one step at a time and see how the method changes geometrically.




Fast browser apps


VisualMath uses Quarto, OJS, Plotly, and math.js, so apps run directly in the browser.




Visual intuition


Plots, tables, and explanations are combined to connect the computation with the underlying mathematics.
